summary: My early interest in microbial genetics began during my graduate studies at the University of Pittsburgh. It was there that I first became fascinated by the intersection of genetics and microbiology. This foundation set the stage for my later work in recombinant DNA research.

During my postdoctoral work at Yale University, I was exposed to bacterial plasmids and gene mapping, which really sparked my interest in DNA recombination. This experience was pivotal in shaping my focus on recombinant DNA technology. Yale provided the environment and resources that allowed me to explore these exciting scientific breakthroughs.

I met Stanley Cohen at a plasmid meeting held in Hawaii in 1972. That meeting was a key moment because it brought us together and led to our collaboration on recombinant DNA experiments. Hawaii was the backdrop for an important turning point in my scientific career.

My collaboration with Stanley Cohen began after we met at a plasmid meeting in Hawaii in 1972. He had expertise in plasmid biology, while I specialized in restriction enzymes, and together we combined these skills to pioneer recombinant DNA technology. Our partnership was crucial in advancing the field by merging prokaryotic and eukaryotic DNA research.

In 1975, the Asilomar Conference on Recombinant DNA was convened to address the significant biohazard concerns surrounding recombinant DNA research. This conference was essential for establishing safety guidelines and a research moratorium to ensure responsible scientific practices. It helped the community navigate the ethical and safety challenges of this new technology.

The National Institutes of Health played a critical role by issuing guidelines that provided a framework for safely conducting recombinant DNA research. These NIH guidelines helped standardize laboratory practices and addressed public concerns, which was vital for the continued development and acceptance of the technology. Their involvement gave the research community a clear path forward.

Genentech was founded in 1976 as a company to develop commercial applications of recombinant DNA technology, including products like somatostatin and human insulin. I co-founded Genentech with Robert Swanson and provided scientific leadership during its early development. Although I did not participate directly in its research to avoid conflicts of interest, my academic work laid the foundation for its success.

Robert Swanson and I co-founded Genentech together in 1976. He was instrumental in the business and entrepreneurial side, while I focused on the scientific leadership. Our partnership was key to bridging the gap between cutting-edge science and commercial biotechnology.

My academic work at the University of California, San Francisco was foundational to the science behind recombinant DNA technology. While I was involved in Genentech's formation, I chose not to engage directly in its research to avoid conflicts of interest. UCSF provided the research environment that enabled many of the breakthroughs that Genentech later commercialized.
